data.title: AI, Energy, Population, and the Reshaping of the World Order
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>AI, energy, and population are the three biggest forces reshaping the world order over the next two to three decades. Why two to three decades? Because these three trends are simultaneously reaching critical points within this window: AI will likely replace most cognitive labor; clean energy will likely replace fossil fuels as the primary source of energy; and a shift in the population landscape is already a certainty. These three forces are simultaneously transforming from trends into reality. This is the change that most people will experience in their lifetime. AI is reshaping social structures and class distribution; energy is reshaping geopolitical order; and population is reshaping the world's center of gravity and power map. The directions of these three variables are clear and irreversible. Their cumulative effect will bring about structural changes in the world. We don't need to predict the specific future; simply understanding the direction of each variable is enough to realize that our accustomed social order, economic logic, and international landscape will undergo profound transformation. AI: Intelligence Oversupply and the Disappearance of the Middle Layer AI is not just another technological upgrade. The steam engine replaced animal power, industrialization replaced manual labor, and computers enabled automation—each previous technological revolution primarily replaced manual and repetitive mechanical labor. AI is the first to massively replace cognitive judgment. A large amount of ordinary mental work—analysis, writing, programming, customer service, translation, basic decision-making—is being supplied infinitely at near-zero marginal cost. This means the market value of mental labor will depreciate significantly. Just as the Industrial Revolution rendered the skills of hand weavers worthless, AI will subject the professional accumulation of a large number of knowledge workers to the same fate. But this time, the speed is faster and the impact is wider—because the occupational scope covered by cognitive labor is far greater than that of any kind of manual labor. The most direct structural consequence of this is the exacerbation of social stratification. The gap between those who can leverage AI to amplify their abilities and those who cannot is not linear, but exponential. While past technological revolutions eliminated old jobs, they also created numerous new supporting jobs. Industrialization required workers, managers, engineers, accountants, and salespeople; the unfolding of the entire industrial chain created a large middle class. AI is different this time. Its characteristic is that a very small number of people can perform tasks that previously required a large workforce. One person plus AI may be more effective than an entire team in the past. AI will certainly create new jobs, but these new jobs are not evenly distributed—they are concentrated at both ends: top-level creative work and bottom-level manual services, precisely where the middle layer is hollowed out. The middle class is not just a concept of income. It is the stable structural foundation of modern society. The middle class has properties to protect, children to educate, and pensions to preserve their value, so they naturally tend towards order, gradualism, and compromise. They are the source of votes for moderates in democratic politics, the main force in the consumer market, and the carriers of social consensus. In fact, this group is already shrinking. In the United States, the percentage of people who identify as middle class fell from 53% in 2008 to 44% in 2014. In Germany, the proportion of the middle class fell from 60% in 1991 to 54% in 2013, and the proportion of middle-class people among young people is declining even faster. This trend occurred before the widespread adoption of AI. The hollowing out of the middle class means that all these functions have lost their bearers. Education is one of the hardest-hit areas. The modern education system is built on an implicit assumption: individuals spend over a decade accumulating knowledge and skills, then recoup that investment over decades of career. This is an investment return model, and it has worked well for the past century. But if AI reduces the market value of most knowledge-based skills to zero, this model collapses. A person spends four years learning accounting, law, and programming, only to graduate to find that AI can perform the same work faster and at a lower cost. Education isn't becoming less important; rather, its function as a social selection mechanism and a pathway to upward social mobility has been significantly weakened. It's harder for children from disadvantaged backgrounds to succeed, not because of unequal educational resources, but because the return on investment in education itself is collapsing. The political consequences of a shrinking middle class are certain: polarization. The middle class fills the middle ground of the political spectrum. With this group shrinking, the middle ground collapses, leaving politics at only two extremes—an angry lower class and an anxious elite—with no buffer or consensus between them. The space for moderate, rational, and compromising politics disappears, replaced by emotional mobilization and identity confrontation. This is not a phenomenon unique to any one country. From the bipartisan polarization in the United States to the rise of the far right in Europe, to the spread of populism around the world, the same structural forces bear similar fruit in different political environments. Political polarization inevitably spills over. Polarized domestic politics naturally tend towards inward contraction and external hardline stances—because this is the only posture acceptable to both sides. Moderate free trade positions lose their political appeal. Each country will develop its own version of economic nationalism. The domestic political consensus upon which globalization rests is crumbling. Meanwhile, AI is also eroding the economic foundations of globalization. The core driver of globalization over the past few decades has been labor cost arbitrage: moving production to places with cheap labor and selling products to places with strong purchasing power. This is a simple yet powerful economic logic that has driven an unprecedented global division of labor. But if AI makes labor costs unimportant, this logic loses its support. The return of manufacturing is no longer a political slogan, but an economic rationale. The political and economic foundations of globalization are being eroded simultaneously by AI. This has a particularly profound impact on developing countries: the effective catch-up path of the past few decades—first absorbing the transfer of manufacturing, and then gradually climbing up the value chain—is being shut down. Energy: The Decline of Fossil Fuels and the Restructuring of Geopolitical Order Energy is the physical foundation of civilization. How much energy a civilization can mobilize determines how much it can accomplish. For the past two centuries, this foundation has been fossil fuels—coal, oil, and natural gas. They shaped industrial systems, urban forms, transportation networks, and also the basic structure of international politics. This foundation is changing. The cost curve for photovoltaic modules is already very clear: it has decreased by nearly 90% in the past decade and is still accelerating. Wind power is following a similar path. Energy storage technology is rapidly iterating. Even without considering nuclear fusion, the strategic scarcity of fossil fuels is being continuously weakened simply by the ongoing cost reduction of renewable energy and energy storage. If nuclear fusion is commercialized during this period, this process will be significantly accelerated. Clean energy is not a supplement to fossil fuels, but a replacement. In twenty or thirty years, we will most likely live in a world where energy costs have dropped dramatically. Many people believe that oil's last bastion is transportation fuels—cars, ships, and airplanes. This is not the case. Electrification has already made oil's position in ground transportation precarious. Synthetic fuels have also begun to be commercialized, and with the continued decline in the cost of clean energy, synthetic fuels will significantly replace petroleum fuels, including the most difficult to replace jet fuel. Oil's true moat is the chemical industry. Globally, plastics, fertilizers, synthetic fibers, synthetic rubber—almost all modern chemical products—start with hydrocarbon molecules produced by petroleum cracking. The chemical industry is the area where oil is "irreplaceable." But this moat is also being breached. Green hydrogen, combined with CO₂ captured from the air, can synthesize methanol. Methanol is an extremely versatile chemical platform molecule, from which major chemical product lines such as olefins, aromatics, plastics, and synthetic fibers can be derived. Bio-based pathways offer another alternative. The key bottleneck is not technology, but cost—currently, green synthesis pathways are far more expensive than oil cracking. But cost depends on electricity prices. As clean energy continues to decrease in cost, artificial synthesis pathways will eventually compete head-on with oil in terms of economics. At that point, oil will truly be downgraded from a strategic resource to an ordinary commodity. It won't be that no one uses it anymore, but that it will no longer be irreplaceable. The geopolitical consequences of this are far-reaching. Fossil fuels are not just energy; they are the underlying operating system of the current international order. The strategic position of the Middle East is built upon oil. The US involvement in the Middle East, the financial foundations of Middle Eastern countries, and the geopolitical importance of the Gulf region all ultimately stem from the fact that the world cannot live without what lies beneath that land. Saudi Arabia still derives over 60% of its fiscal revenue from oil. Russia's great power status is similarly highly dependent on energy exports—oil and natural gas have long contributed 30-50% of its federal budget revenue, are the Kremlin's most important single source of income, and are its primary tool for exerting influence over Europe. The dollar's reserve currency status is largely supported by the dollar-denominated pricing mechanism of oil trade. When the strategic value of oil significantly diminishes, all of this must be repriced. For countries whose fiscal revenue depends on oil for over 60%, this is not merely an economic adjustment, but a shattering of the very foundation of their national survival. The Middle East faces not an economic transformation problem, but an existential crisis—the wealth and status accumulated over generations are built on a resource that is depreciating. The same applies to Russia. Without the leverage of energy, Russia's bargaining power in international competition will shrink dramatically. Global trade will also be dismantled from the supply side. Currently, fossil fuels and their derivatives account for a huge proportion of global maritime trade. The essential characteristic of clean energy is localization—sunlight and wind do not need to be transported across oceans, and power plants generate electricity wherever they are built. Energy localization means a significant reduction in the interdependence between countries formed through energy trade. Trade is not just the flow of goods, but also the adhesive between countries. When this adhesive weakens, international relations will become looser and more unpredictable. Thus, two separate lines—AI and energy—converge on the same conclusion. AI eliminates the driving force of global division of labor from the demand side: labor arbitrage no longer makes economic sense. Energy eliminates the necessity of global trade from the supply side: the most important commodities no longer need to be transported across borders. Two completely different variables, with their own independent logical chains, point in the same direction: the form of globalization of the past few decades is coming to an end. Population: The Southward Shift of the World's Center of Gravity Population is the most certain of the three variables. While AI development may encounter technological bottlenecks and energy pathways may experience unexpected breakthroughs or delays, the future of population is almost entirely unpredictable. This is because those who will determine the labor force and population structure two or three decades from now have already been born today—or are already certain they will not be born. Once the fertility rate falls below a certain threshold, it won't recover within decades. This isn't a policy issue, but rather a matter of social inertia. Almost no country has successfully reversed a continuously declining fertility rate. The global population landscape two or three decades from now is largely determined today: the labor force in Europe and East Asia will experience a precipitous decline. According to UN population projections, China's working-age population will decrease from approximately 980 million in 2024 to approximately 750 million in 2050, a reduction of nearly a quarter. Japan and South Korea are entering a period of deep aging. Meanwhile, the young populations of South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Africa will continue to grow. India's working-age population will increase by approximately 140 million during the same period, making it the world's largest labor market. Africa's population will double, with several African countries entering a period of demographic dividend. The global population center of gravity will clearly shift from the north to the south. Population growth itself is a driving force. More people mean more demand, more transactions, more trial and error, and more possibilities. A society with a majority of young people, if provided with basic economic opportunities, becomes a breeding ground for entrepreneurship and risk-taking, and the entire society moves forward; otherwise, the same demographic energy may translate into instability. An aging society is the opposite—no matter how advanced the system, it is permeated with a defensive temperament and a mindset of preservation. Culture certainly has an influence, but generational structure is a more fundamental variable. Over the past two centuries, this driving force has undergone several major shifts. In the 19th century, this shift occurred in Europe; in the first half of the 20th century, it moved to North America; and in the second half, it shifted to East Asia. Each shift was accompanied by a reshaping of the global landscape. In the next two or three decades, this momentum will shift to South Asia and Africa. This is not a prediction, but an extension of an already occurring fact on the timeline. In the AI ​​era, the form of the demographic dividend has fundamentally changed. The past demographic dividend was "cheap labor"—a large number of young people willing to work in factories at low wages, providing a cost advantage for global manufacturing. This dividend is indeed depreciating in the face of AI. However, the new form of the demographic dividend has not disappeared; on the contrary, it has become even more important. Population means a consumer market—as income levels rise, South Asia and Africa will contribute the vast majority of global consumption growth. Population means a talent pool—extreme talent is a probabilistic event; the larger the population base, the greater the absolute number of geniuses at the far right of the distribution. However, whether this potential can be realized still depends on whether basic education and the institutional environment can identify and cultivate geniuses. Population also signifies the fiscal tax base—the total amount of resources a nation can mobilize ultimately depends on how many people are creating and exchanging value. Whoever possesses the largest market has the strongest demand attraction; whoever has the largest talent pool is most likely to generate leading innovations—provided the institutional environment allows talent to emerge. Conversely, the predicament of aging countries is deeper than it appears on the surface. Population decline not only means labor shortages—which can perhaps be partially offset by AI—but also a continued contraction in domestic demand. All economic models of the past few decades have been based on market expansion. After the population declines, it's not a slowdown in growth, but rather the failure of the growth paradigm itself. Japan's experience over the past thirty years has been caused by policy mistakes, but more fundamentally by a struggle against the irreversible physical reality of its population structure. Pension and healthcare expenditures will consume an increasingly larger proportion of public finances, squeezing out investment in education, infrastructure, and defense. National capabilities are locked up precisely when transformation is most needed—not only fiscally, but also psychologically: aging societies tend towards conservatism and rigidity, losing the capacity for risk-taking precisely when they most need to embrace change. Moreover, a more fundamental fact should not be overlooked: South Asia and Africa still have a significant backlog of industrialization and modernization to catch up on. The urbanization of billions of people, infrastructure development, the establishment of manufacturing from scratch, and the improvement of public service systems—historical processes already completed in developed countries—are only just beginning or far from finished in these southern countries. Simply catching up on these lessons is enough to unleash enormous economic growth potential. This growth is not dependent on globalization channels, but rather endogenous and structural. Even without considering changes in political systems, industrialization and modernization alone can bring about profound changes in these regions that will last for decades. However, endogenous industrialization has its limits. To leap to global power, domestic demand alone is insufficient. And precisely at this point, a tremendous tension exists. Population growth is shifting southward, but the channels for receiving this growth are closing. In the past, East Asia successfully transformed its demographic dividend into economic growth through the channels provided by globalization—cheap labor integrated into the global division of labor through export processing, gradually accumulating capital and technology. But this channel is being blocked by multiple forces: AI has eliminated the economic logic of labor arbitrage, energy localization has weakened the cohesion of international trade, and political polarization has closed the door to immigration. Southern countries have the momentum for growth, but may not find an outlet to release it. A historical misalignment has emerged between the driving force and the channel. Looking back at each past shift of growth—from Europe to North America, from North America to East Asia—the receiving countries took decades to truly rise, experiencing war, revolution, and institutional reconstruction. There has never been a smooth transition. This round in South Asia and Africa will likely be no exception. Whether a new way can be found to transform the enormous population energy into national power and international influence is the greatest uncertainty in the population variable, and a key issue determining the future world power landscape. Conclusion These three variables, each irreversible and evolving independently, simultaneously affect the same world. AI eliminates the middle layer, energy eliminates resource dependence, and population reshapes the underlying distribution of national power. Every pillar of the current international order—the globalized trade system, the fossil fuel-dominated geopolitical landscape, the structural advantages of northern countries, and the social stability supported by the middle class—will be redefined. These three variables also share a common direction: they all weaken the power of the "center." AI weakens the advantage of large organizations over small teams, energy localization weakens the control of resource-exporting countries over importing countries, and the collapse of globalization allows power to be withdrawn from the global system to regions and countries. The world order of the past two centuries has been built on the logic of centralization—large factories, large corporations, large countries, and a unified global market. These three variables simultaneously undermine the very logic of centralization itself. There's no need to try to predict the final state. But some things are certain: everything we take for granted today—globalization, middle-class society, the fossil fuel order, the North-dominated world order—is not a steady state on a two- or three-decade timescale. Understanding the direction of each of these three lines is enough to re-examine all our certainties today.</div></div>

data.title: When companies stop buying BTC: Is Strategy the only one struggling to stay afloat?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Over the past month, a highly unusual phenomenon has emerged in the Bitcoin market.

Compared to the crypto treasury boom of a few months ago, BTC has now moved beyond a state of mass buying.

Buyers are basically limited to Strategy.

I. Only Strategy is still buying

According to CryptoQuant's analysis, Bitcoin treasury demand is currently entirely driven by Strategy. In the past 30 days, Strategy bought 45,000 Bitcoins, while other companies combined bought only about 1,000, a 99% decrease from before, and their share of total purchases dropped to 2%, indicating that new demand has almost completely disappeared. Currently, Strategy holds about 76% of the Bitcoin treasury share, indicating a highly concentrated industry and a lack of broad corporate demand.

Strategy bought BTC 4 times in March, with the most recent purchase on March 23.

...

Currently holding 762,099 BTC, ranking first in holdings and accounting for 3.629% of the total supply of 21 million.

Furthermore, seemingly to continue accumulating BTC, Strategy announced a new $21 billion STRC equity offering and a new $21 billion MSTR equity offering on March 23. The total financing amount reaches a staggering $42 billion.

Strategy's revised ATM equity offering plan allows it to gradually sell more shares to the public market, instead of raising smaller amounts of capital from external investors through convertible bonds as before.

Strategy's revised ATM equity offering plan allows it to gradually sell more shares to the public market, rather than raising smaller amounts of capital from external investors through convertible bonds as before.

Strategy's preferred shares, such as STRC and STRK, pay monthly dividends to investors, while enabling Strategy to increase its Bitcoin holdings without issuing additional MSTR common stock. [Image of Strategy's preferred shares] Corporate buying of BTC has evolved from multiple companies buying together to almost exclusively Strategy buying, with corporate BTC treasury demand almost vanishing instantly. CryptoQuant's Head of Research, Julio Moreno, pointed out at the beginning of the year: "Most new corporate Bitcoin buyers only made one or two purchases before trading stopped, failing to provide sustained price support." Compared to the expansion phase of 2023-2024, year-over-year BTC demand growth has slowed significantly. Currently, this indicator is below the historical trend line, indicating that capital is contracting rather than the adoption wave that usually drives bull markets.

II. Why are other companies not buying?

1. Declining Risk Appetite

The expectation of further interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve is uncertain. With current high interest rates, the cost of capital is high, and stable returns can be obtained through US Treasury bonds. Therefore, crypto asset allocation has become a non-preferred option.

In addition, the global economic downturn and frequent geopolitical conflicts have led most companies to adopt conservative financial strategies.

2. Crypto Market Turns Bearish

In 2025, a large number of cryptocurrency treasury companies emerged, providing Wall Street investors with another way to invest in cryptocurrencies. As Bitcoin continued to climb, reaching its peak in October, the stock prices of many companies also rose. However, the overall decline in the crypto market subsequently impacted the valuations of these companies.

MoreMarkets co-founder and CEO Altan Tutar made a pessimistic prediction about crypto treasury companies last year: "Most Bitcoin treasury companies will disappear like other DATs." After October last year, the crypto market began its downward trend, with new demand slowing and even showing signs of "demand exhaustion." For most companies, crypto treasury reserves are no longer a good option; volatility and the downward trend have directly shaken the confidence of most companies in buying them. Previously, many companies hoarded various cryptocurrencies because it could create a positive cycle. After issuing shares, they would buy a certain coin; holding that coin would attract more market attention, thus driving up the stock price; after raising funds, they would continue to buy… This self-sustaining model requires a consistently bull market, but when the crypto market is in a downturn, this model becomes ineffective. A market downturn leads to a reduction in a company's book profits, which in turn weakens investor confidence, putting pressure on stock prices and further impacting the company's financing capabilities. Ultimately, the company lacks spare cash to buy more… For example, at the end of February, Ethereum treasury FG Nexus reduced its holdings by 7,550 ETH, worth approximately $14.06 million. The reason for the reduction was losses: the company had purchased 50,600 ETH during the DAT company boom in August-September 2025, at an average price of approximately $3,940, for a total value of $200 million. Now, FG Nexus has accumulated losses of $86.98 million due to its ETH investments. When the price of BTC continues to fluctuate downwards, many crypto treasury companies will stop buying cryptocurrencies and instead favor more conservative cash and low-risk asset management. Companies flocked to the market during the bull market and then scattered during the bear market. Stable buyers became cyclical participants. III. Why is Strategy still a seasoned player? While most companies choose to exit the market, Strategy is a notable exception. First, for most companies, BTC and other cryptocurrencies are part of their asset allocation, but for Strategy, BTC is the source of the company's valuation logic, a core asset, and the foundation of its narrative—Strategy has consistently played the role of a BTC bull in both bull and bear markets. Second, for Strategy, BTC has become a belief. Michael Saylor positions Bitcoin as "Digital Capital," calling it the ultimate reserve asset of the 21st century. On March 22, Saylor stated, "The Orange March continues." Despite the market crash last weekend causing a 10% loss on the company's Bitcoin investments, the company still increased its Bitcoin holdings.

Finally, Strategy is deeply tied to BTC. Investors buying Strategy's stock are essentially buying BTC exposure. Strategy has essentially transformed itself into an ETF with a leveraged structure. Therefore, when the market is down, Strategy will continue to increase its holdings.

In summary, even if the overall crypto market is unfavorable, driven by long-term faith, counter-cyclical averaging down, and a continued strengthening of its BTC exposure, Strategy will continue to buy BTC even when the market is declining.

In conclusion, even if the overall crypto market is unfavorable, driven by long-term faith, counter-cyclical averaging down, and a continued strengthening of its BTC exposure, Strategy will continue to buy BTC even when the market is down.

Bernstein analysts believe that "Strategy plays the role of 'Bitcoin's last safe-haven bank,' and Bitcoin ETFs are attracting more resilient (and less speculative) funding sources. Bitcoin's robust capital base is growing." IV. The Impact of Strategy's "Lone Star" In the short term, Strategy's continued buying will have a positive effect on BTC prices. When other companies stop buying, Strategy's stable buying will alleviate market selling pressure and prevent a precipitous drop in BTC prices. However, this support is also affected by Strategy: factors such as lower-than-expected fundraising and slower accumulation will weaken Strategy's ability to accumulate, which may further lead to a decline in BTC prices. In the long term, the increasing concentration of BTC in the hands of a single company is detrimental to the market's resilience. Once Strategy adjusts its strategy, it will further affect confidence in the crypto market. Strategy is no longer just a participant, but a key variable affecting the stability of the crypto market.

data.title: Understanding the US SEC's Token Classification System
data.description:
Author: Jack Inabinet Source: Bankless Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Last week, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) jointly released landmark regulatory guidance. This long-awaited joint interpretation details under what circumstances and in what ways federal securities laws apply to crypto assets.

The crypto industry has long existed in a legal gray area, and this new framework attempts to eliminate some of the uncertainty with a token classification system, helping to define under what conditions digital assets will be considered securities.

Today we'll break down this joint guidance and speculate on how it will ultimately classify mainstream digital assets.

According to the SEC's definition and guidance, "digital goods" refer to crypto assets whose value derives from their functional role in the crypto ecosystem and from market supply and demand. This broad definition covers utility tokens on numerous mature blockchain networks. The SEC has explicitly listed 16 crypto assets that fall into this category, including: Aptos (APT), Avalanche (AVAX), Bitcoin (BTC), Bitcoin Cash (BCH), Cardano (ADA), Chainlink (LINK), Dogecoin (DOGE), Ether (ETH), Hedera (HBAR), Litecoin (LTC), Polkadot (DOT), Shiba Inu (SHIB), Solana (SOL), Stellar (XLM), Tezos (XTZ), and XRP. While the SEC did not explain why each of these cryptocurrencies was classified as a non-security digital commodity, the guidance emphasizes that they do not meet the legal definition of a "security" and therefore will not be automatically considered securities. The SEC states that its use of the term "digital commodity" is strictly based on economics and is used to describe the typical characteristics of a class of non-security digital assets. The guidance also points out that, apart from stablecoins that meet the requirements of the GENIUS Act, any crypto asset that meets the definition of a "commodity" under the Commodity Exchange Act can be considered a true commodity and thus subject to specific regulation by the CFTC. Popular tokens not mentioned by the SEC but potentially classified as "digital goods" also include: BONK: Solana ecosystem meme coin, which has gradually developed governance and various practical functions since its launch. CC: Canton blockchain native utility token used for trading, settlement, data synchronization, and asset transfer. EIGEN: EigenLayer ecosystem restaking utility token used to provide economic security. ETC: Ethereum's proof-of-work version, already recognized by the SEC as a non-security digital commodity. HYPE: Hyperliquid network native gas token. STX: Stacks Bitcoin Layer 2 network native gas and staking reward token. SUI: Sui network native gas and staking token. TAO: Bittensor network native token used to incentivize machine learning contributions in the decentralized AI market. TRX: Tron network native gas and staking token. XMR: Monero network native token, emphasizing privacy, used to pay transaction fees and maintain network security. alt="2u58iCrYkWPFAZ8bvUi6u7gDiJlfnpnIltYjyuua.png">

Digital Collectibles

Former SEC Chairman Gary Gensler was once asked by Congress whether tokenized Pokémon trading cards were securities. He did not give a clear answer at the time, only stating that more information was needed to determine this.

Now, the crypto industry seems to have finally received the long-awaited conclusion.

The SEC's latest proposed guidance indicates that tokenized Pokémon trading cards could be classified as "digital collectibles," but more information is needed before their non-securities status is formally confirmed.

This category applies to crypto assets intended for collection purposes, including assets representing or transferring artwork, music, videos, trading cards, in-game items, and rights related to memes as previously defined.

Similar to physical collectibles, digital collectibles do not grant holders any corporate ownership, even if the holder owns the commercialization rights to the underlying intellectual property, and are therefore inherently non-securities. The SEC explicitly classifies CryptoPunks, Chromie Squiggles, fan tokens, WIF, and VCOIN as digital collectibles. While the SEC categorizes memecoins as digital collectibles, it also specifically states that once these tokens become functional within the associated crypto ecosystem, they may be converted into digital goods. Furthermore, the SEC explicitly points out that, similar to physical collectibles, most fragmented collectibles are highly likely to constitute securities because buyers rely on third-party management. For example, providing physical custody of Pokémon cards for tokenized versions might constitute "management," thus triggering registration requirements under federal securities laws. Popular projects not mentioned by the SEC but currently potentially fitting the definition of "digital collectibles" include: Beeple's "The First 5000 Days": The most expensive NFT in history, valued for its cultural significance, artist reputation, and historical standing in the NFT market. PEPE: Internet cultural meme coins, valued for their community sentiment and reach. Pudgy Penguins and PENGU: Community-driven NFTs and token brands centered around the penguin IP. Quantum Cats: On-chain digital collectibles based on Bitcoin inscriptions, valued for their scarcity and status within the Bitcoin ecosystem. Saga Monkes: Featuring Solana Saga. Limited-edition NFTs linked to mobile phones possess collectible value due to their scarcity, early holder status, and ecosystem connections. Digital tools are crypto assets designed to achieve specific functions. They possess intrinsic utility value, and many are soulbound (SBT) in form, non-transferable, and permanently bound to a single user. Importantly, the SEC explicitly stipulates that digital tools cannot possess inherent economic attributes or rights, such as generating passive income, enjoying rights to future corporate revenue, profits, or asset distributions. The SEC listed various use cases for digital tools, including memberships, tickets, credentials, property documents, identity badges, etc., and categorized Ethereum Name Service (ENS) and CoinDesk's "Microcosms" NFT consensus conference tickets as digital tools. Other digital assets that may qualify as “digital instruments” include: FanDome NFT: Comic-themed NFTs distributed free to attendees at the 2021 DC Fan Virtual Convention. POAP: NFT digital badges proving participation in offline/online events. Propy NFT: On-chain digital certificate of legal ownership of real estate. VeeFriends: A series of NFTs created by Gary Vaynerchuk, usable to unlock exclusive access to events such as VCON. World ID: Soul-bound “proof of identity” credentials issued by the Ultraman Sam World project. The SEC's definition of non-security stablecoins is very concise and straightforward: Payment stablecoins as defined by the GENIUS Act, which will take effect next January, will be considered non-security stablecoins. Therefore, the SEC will not impose registration requirements on payment stablecoins that comply with the Act at the issuance and redemption stages (although these stablecoins will still be subject to full regulation by other U.S. regulatory agencies). The guidelines also clarify that, prior to the enactment of the GENIUS Act, **"covered stablecoins"** under the existing definition remain non-securities and not subject to SEC regulation. To meet this definition, stablecoin holders are not entitled to any interest, profits, or other returns, and the reserve assets must consist entirely of US dollars and/or other low-risk, highly liquid assets. While the SEC did not name specific currencies, the following dollar-pegged assets meet the definition of "covered stablecoins": PYUSD: A stablecoin issued by PayPal and custodied by Paxos. USAT: Tether's latest dollar stablecoin, designed to comply with the GENIUS Act. USDC: Issued by Circle, over-collateralized 1:1 with risk-free dollar assets. KlarnaUSD: A stablecoin initiated by Klarna, a buy-now-pay-later giant, and issued by Bridge, a subsidiary of Stripe. The most impactful, yet also the most ambiguous, part of the SEC guidance is the definition of digital securities. The SEC's stance is firm: any digital asset that fails the Howe test is a security. This determination has historically been accompanied by lengthy legal battles. The SEC also classifies tokenized versions of products already explicitly classified as securities, such as tokenized money market funds, stocks, and other structured investment products, as digital securities. However, this does not mean that all "wrapped" tokens are securities. The SEC explains that protocol mining, staking, and wrapping non-security crypto assets do not constitute the issuance or sale of securities; the guidance further clarifies that some airdrops do not meet the "money investment" requirement under the Howe test. Nevertheless, the SEC still cannot give a straightforward answer to under what circumstances a digital asset becomes a security. In each individual case, the specific facts and scenarios of how the project promotes the asset will determine whether it constitutes an investment contract (i.e., a security). Crypto assets that were originally non-security assets will be considered securities if they are issued or sold through an investment contract that meets the Howe test. Furthermore, even if a non-security crypto asset is initially issued in the form of an investment contract, it will not permanently be considered a security; when the original investment contract is fulfilled, or the project fails to deliver on its promised development and functionality, the asset can revert to its non-security status. The SEC did not specify which assets qualify as securities. However, it's worth noting that despite a court ruling that some of XRP's sales constituted illegal investment contracts, the SEC guidance still classifies it as a non-securities "digital commodity." While this guidance represents a step towards regulatory clarity, it actually reinforces the ambiguity regarding securities classification, merely extending existing federal securities laws to digital assets. This also exposes projects promising undeveloped features or future utility to renewed regulatory risks. Although digital commodities, digital collectibles, digital tools, and stablecoins are inherently non-securities, if issued or sold through investment contracts, the project developers are still subject to federal securities laws.

data.title: Is AI pricing emerging from its "locked room"? Bittensor provides the answer.
data.description:
Author: Prathik Desai Source: tokendispatch Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The AI ​​industry today resembles a closed religious system: fundraising and valuations are conducted behind closed doors. A few leading companies raise huge sums of money, recruit top researchers, and rent massive computing clusters, while the market can only infer their value through funding announcements that are only released every few months. The so-called "valuation" is often just a number agreed upon by a few people in the room, rather than the true price discovered by a freely flowing market. By the time ordinary investors can see the price, most of the potential for growth has already been devoured by early participants.

Bittensor's core argument is: AI should not be funded in this way. I am deeply fascinated by the system it is building.

Bittensor's core argument is: AI should not be funded in this way. I am fascinated by the system it is building.

It's not because it can create models superior to those of OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google—at least not yet—but because it has found a decentralized path to publicly evaluate, fund, and price AI projects before they grow into traditional companies. This model is drastically different from many decentralized attempts in previous waves of AI development. Bittensor's subnet system continuously supports teams, rewards high-performing performers, eliminates lagging projects, and reprices the entire AI ecosystem in real time. This is an unprecedented way to price AI. I admit that this model is brutal in its AI development process, but it is also more honest. In this in-depth analysis, I will break down Bittensor's operating logic and why it may be more advantageous than any previous AI pricing attempt. The Secret Chamber of AI Pricing In the first quarter of 2025 alone, AI startups raised $73.1 billion, accounting for 58% of global venture capital funding. Despite warnings from investment firms like GIC and TPG that valuations in some niche sectors are excessively high, there is virtually no operational performance to support these valuations. This model benefits founders, insiders, and later-stage investors, but excludes others: those providing key computing power, developers building on open-source models, and early adopters are all excluded from the benefits. Even the rise of open-source AI has failed to change this situation; funding remains concentrated in cloud service contracts, deployment layers, enterprise packaging, technical support, security, and distribution. Throughout the value creation process, the public widely participates and contributes, but the fruits are reaped by only a few. While this pattern has existed for a long time, the real change comes from the rise of the open-source model AI economy. Red Hat developers noted in their report that enterprises are increasingly adopting open-source AI models for localized deployment, autonomous control, and specialized tasks, especially in highly regulated industries such as telecommunications and banking. Enterprises need AI deployment solutions that can be monitored, automated, and scaled, not just references to a single AI model. Large institutions like McKinsey also agree with this trend. Their research shows that over half of the surveyed companies have fully implemented open-source AI in their technology stacks. The survey covered over 700 technical leaders and senior developers in 41 countries. Bittensor's model challenges the current pricing system for AI projects based on these industry changes. Crypto-native investors are going crazy for Bittensor's native token, TAO, whose price has doubled in the past month. Others are keen to debate the merits of decentralized versus centralized AI. But for me, the more important thing is to explore a more precise way to price AI. Bittensor's answer is: to bring together all parties involved in funding, developing, validating, and using AI in the same market, pricing AI based on public metrics. Bringing AI to the Public Market It's easy to understand if you think of Bittensor as a network of micro-AI economies rather than a single token. Each subnet is a specialized marketplace for a specific task within the AI ​​technology stack, potentially focusing on inference, distributed training, predictive signals, or computing power supply. Subnet creators set incentive mechanisms and target tasks, miners execute these tasks, validators score the results, and stakers support specific validators by staking TAO. Following Bittensor's dynamic TAO upgrade in February 2025, the incentive mechanism became more innovative: each subnet had its own dedicated token and liquidity pool. Bittensor was no longer just a single, generalized AI investment target, but rather an ecosystem encompassing numerous small AI projects. In the second half of 2025, Bittensor further linked reward distribution to net TAO inflows rather than a rigid token price. In December of the same year, TAO completed its first halving, reducing the daily issuance to 3,600 tokens, further forcing capital to allocate resources selectively and turning the AI ​​market into an arena for survival of the fittest. Web3 researcher and author Jeff aptly summarized this as the "dynamic mechanism of Darwinian AI," and provided a brilliant summary in the 0xJeff newsletter: The core of Darwinism is natural selection and survival of the fittest; individuals compete with each other, and desirable traits for survival are passed down from generation to generation. Similar logic is implemented across multiple layers of Bittensor: Subnet Competition: Subnets compete for a daily incentive share of 3600 TAO tokens. Top subnets gain a longer survival period through incentives. Miner Competition: Miners compete to provide the best results. Global participants compare performance based on key subnet metrics. Top miners receive 41% of the subnet's alpha token rewards. Validator and Investor Competition: Validators compete to validate miner tasks, while investors compete to bet on the best-performing subnet. What happens if you don't participate in the competition or perform poorly? Elimination. The subnet will be directly delisted (yes, deleted directly from the system). This is precisely the core difference between it and the traditional AI model. In the traditional model, founders promote the company, raise equity, build a team, develop internal infrastructure, and hope the market will recognize its valuation. Bittensor disrupts this model by publicly announcing its investment projects early in the market. In this model, entrepreneurs first launch a subnet, followed by GPU operators contributing computing resources. Then, developers and researchers contribute their work, and investors purchase investment shares via TAO or specific subnet tokens. Finally, customers pay for the underlying services. Ultimately, the market considers all factors and prices the project holistically. What I like most about it is that it reimagines the capital market for every stakeholder. Unlike private startups, investors can continuously discover prices without waiting for the next round of funding. In fact, Bittensor allows developers to view the entire ecosystem through the TAO platform or focus their investments more precisely on individual subnets they favor. For developers, the appeal lies in participating in the positive impact of AI development without being confined to Anthropico, OpenAI, or other elite hyperscale data centers. It provides entrepreneurs with a capital market around their ideas, supporting them even before they develop into mature companies—something unprecedented in the venture capital industry. This is evident in how capital is pooled within the network. A few subnets are currently attracting disproportionate TAO funding inflows and outflows, while others lag behind. The top five subnets by market capitalization account for nearly a third of the total market capitalization of the 128 subnets. For customers, the system offers cheaper and more flexible access to open infrastructure. In addition, the Bittensor model is more appealing to all stakeholders because it not only sounds fairer but is also more commercially viable. The market is maturing. Institutional investors are increasingly viewing Bittensor as a compliant and investable asset, a trend that is evident. In December 2025, the Grayscale Bittensor Trust was listed on the OTCQX premium over-the-counter market, providing traditional investors with a familiar channel to participate in this unfamiliar yet highly sought-after asset. A hallmark of any maturing emerging market is the availability of compliant packaging, trading codes, on-screen quotes, and brokerage account access—the path taken by Bitcoin, Ethereum ETFs, and Digital Asset Bonds (DATs). While Bittensor may not have the same level of recognition in the crypto market as Bitcoin and Ethereum, the launch of the Grayscale Trust signifies a shift in institutional interest from theory to tangible products. Bittensor's work has even garnered recognition from top figures in the elite industries it is poised to disrupt. When prominent venture capitalist and entrepreneur Chamath Palihapitiya mentioned Bittensor's distributed training runtime to Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, Huang didn't dismiss it as a mere low-level achievement in the cryptocurrency space. He called it a "modern-day Folding@home," referring to a decentralized, distributed project that utilizes the surplus computing power of volunteer computers to simulate protein folding or other complex problems. This positioning places Bittensor within the long-term historical context of distributed computing, rather than limiting it to the narrative of token cycles. The recent achievements of Templar, one of Bittensor's leading subnets, further validate its capabilities from a technical perspective: its Covenant-72B is a massive model with 72 billion parameters, trained from scratch using 1.1 trillion tokens through collaboration among more than 20 participants worldwide via Bittensor. In public benchmark tests, Covenant-72B achieved an MMLU score of 67.11, higher than LLaMA-2-70B's 65.63. To put it bluntly, it currently cannot surpass OpenAI or Anthropic, but it is sufficient to prove that decentralized collaboration can build commercially valuable AI infrastructure. Subnets like Chutes are explicitly positioned as decentralized serverless AI computing power platforms, and Bittensor's official documentation also defines subnets as independent competitive markets for digital goods such as inference and training. This indicates that the market is not pricing in a vague AI narrative, but rather pricing in independent modules of the technology stack. The Dilemma on the Demand Side: Bittensor's supply-side transparency far exceeds any other AI market: data such as issuance volume, staking flow, and subnets where capital is concentrated are readily available. The real problem lies in the lack of transparency on the demand side. Blockchain only records token transfers but doesn't collect data on user retention, API usage quality, profit margins, audited revenue, etc. Even if a subnet appears commercially prosperous, investors often can only infer business quality through market structure, rather than based on financial statements. Pine Analytics, in its analyses such as "Transparent Supply vs. Opaque Demand" and "Chutes (SN64): Low Prices Supported by Subsidies," offered sharp criticism: Bittensor's impressive commercial performance may still be driven by subsidies, which are essentially TAO issuance rewards within subnets. Pine's calculations show that the network's verifiable external revenue remains negligible compared to the value implied by the TAO price. The most typical example is Bittensor's largest subnet, Chutes: it receives $52 million in TAO issuance subsidies annually, but only generates $2.4 million in external revenue. Without subsidies, its operating costs would be prohibitively high. This is not to deny the Bittensor model, but simply to illustrate that the current market is pricing in the vision of AI rather than its cash flow. This is precisely why I pay particular attention to Bittensor's development. It possesses all the signs of a mature ecosystem. While it hasn't ended the debate on "decentralized AI" and is still refining the most accurate valuation methods for AI projects, it has already taken a significant step in revealing a problem that the private market has long deliberately ignored: pricing belief and valuation through the public market. While private AI giants demand the world believe that a few people in a room determine trillions of dollars in valuations, Bittensor chooses to trust the public market. I know the latter isn't perfect, but I appreciate and endorse the transparency it brings.

data.title: Is Bittensor the holy grail of decentralized AI or a castle in the air built on subsidies?
data.description:
Author: Clow,Plain Language Blockchain

In March 2026, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said something on the "All-In" podcast that immediately sent a crypto project trending.

He said Bittensor is a "modern version of Folding@home."

How much is that statement worth?

The price of TAO surged from $243 before the podcast to $365, a monthly increase of over 100%. Open interest in futures contracts ballooned to $639 million, compared to less than $132 million at the beginning of March. Grayscale had already submitted its S-1 application for a TAO spot ETF to the SEC at the end of December 2025, with Bitwise following suit on the same day. But what truly unsettled those in the industry wasn't the price, but something that happened on March 10th. Bittensor's subnet 3 (Templar) announced that it had trained a 72 billion-parameter language model using over 70 ordinary computers scattered around the world, connected via an average 500 Mb/s home broadband connection. This model, called Covenant-72B, achieved a score of 67.1 on the MMLU benchmark—approaching or even surpassing Meta's LLaMA-2-70B, trained using a cluster of supercomputers. A group of individual users' graphics cards trained a large-scale model that rivals Silicon Valley giants. Even more bizarrely, no one knows where these nodes are located. They might be in a rented apartment in Tokyo, in a hacker's basement in Berlin, or even in your next-door neighbor's house. All contribution records and reward distributions are written on the Bittensor blockchain—transparent, immutable, and permissionless. This isn't just a story of "cheap computing power." It's a war about "who has the right to train AI." What is it doing—128 smart factories operating simultaneously? The success of Covenant-72B isn't based on brute force, but on an algorithm called SparseLoCo. In short, this algorithm compresses the training data that needs to be transmitted between nodes by 146 times, achieving a compression rate of over 97%, while maintaining almost no loss of model accuracy. This means you no longer need a multi-billion dollar supercomputing center like Nvidia's; a 500 Mbps home broadband connection is sufficient. But Bittensor does much more than just train a single model. It currently has 128 concurrently running subnets, each performing a different task. You can think of the entire network as a giant industrial park: Factory 3 (Templar) is responsible for training large models; Factory 64 (Chutes) provides inference services, reportedly with over 400,000 users and processing 5 million requests daily, at only 15% of AWS's price; Factory 4 (Targon) handles confidential GPU computing, and Intel engineers even co-authored a technical white paper on trusted execution environments with it—not just a business collaboration with a logo, but two Intel engineers who actually put their names on it; Factory 51 (Lium) is the GPU P2P market, reportedly managing a cluster of over 500 H100 computing power. The head effect is very obvious—the top ten subnets take 56% of the network's emissions rewards. But the economic mechanism itself is even more noteworthy. After November 2025, Bittensor launched the Taoflow model: the amount of TAO rewards each subnet receives is no longer determined by a vote of 64 validators, but directly by the flow of market funds. Rewards go to the subnets where funds flow. An 86.8-day exponential moving average automatically adjusts, and subnets with continuous net outflows will be "starved." Even more ingenious is the dTAO mechanism—each subnet now has its own Alpha token. Investors stake TAO to obtain Alpha from specific subnets, essentially betting on a specific vertical direction. According to v1 research data, as of March, the total market capitalization of all subnet Alpha tokens was approximately $1.12 billion, accounting for about 27% of TAO's own market capitalization. In simpler terms: Bittensor is not an AI product, but a smart production protocol that allows idle GPUs worldwide to automatically team up, compete, and be eliminated. Why it matters—not "cheap," but "sovereignty." On the surface, Bittensor's selling point is that "decentralized computing power is cheaper than centralized power." But this claim doesn't hold up to scrutiny—we'll explain why later. The core of what Jensen Huang said in his podcast wasn't actually price. He said the future of AI isn't about OpenAI dominating, but rather "A and B coexisting": proprietary models and open-source/distributed models will run in parallel for a long time. The reason is simple—fields like healthcare, defense, and manufacturing require complete control over AI models, and this control can only come from open-source or distributed architectures. At the GTC conference, Jensen Huang proposed the concept of a "token factory"—in the future, not only text will be tokenized, but protein structures, robot movements, and physical simulations will also be tokenized. Every scientific field will need its own "intelligence generator." Bittensor's 128 subnets are precisely the prototype of this vision. But a deeper reason is: what if US regulations suddenly tighten, what if a country blocks OpenAI's API, what if your industry is prohibited from using certain closed-source models—what will you do? Bittensor provides an AI infrastructure that is "censorship-resistant." Model weights are open source, nodes are globally distributed, and no single point can be shut down. When Intel engineers chose to co-publish the white paper with Targon, and when early Uber investor Jason Calacanis predicted TAO's 200x growth potential and specifically established the Stillcore Capital fund to bet on TAO and its subnet tokens, they weren't attracted by its "cheapness." They were attracted by the fact that this might be the only AI network that can still function under extreme circumstances. What Bittensor is really selling isn't computing power, it's sovereignty. Doesn't anyone see a problem with this? Now let's look at the other side of the coin. TAO currently has a market capitalization of approximately $3.9 billion. But its most successful subnet, Chutes, only generates $1.3 million to $2.4 million in annualized external revenue. Meanwhile, Chutes receives $52 million worth of TAO subsidies annually from the protocol. Let's calculate the subsidy ratio: 22:1 to 40:1. For every $1 a user pays, the network issues $22 to $40 worth of TAO through inflation to subsidize miners. If these subsidies were removed, Chutes' claim of "15% of the AWS price" would instantly reverse to "1.6 to 3.5 times the AWS price." The network's externally confirmed annual revenue is between $3 million and $15 million, corresponding to a revenue multiple of 175 to 400 times. This figure for traditional high-growth SaaS companies typically doesn't exceed 50 times. So why is the market buying in? Three things: First, TAO's total supply cap of 21 million tokens and its halving mechanism make it as scarce as Bitcoin; second, the expectation of a Grayscale ETF, which, if approved, will open up channels for institutional funds; and third, the narrative itself of being "the only decentralized AI protocol capable of running large models." On-chain data also tells a bullish story. Exchanges saw a net outflow of $5.77 million on March 21-22—large investors were accumulating tokens. According to OnchainLens monitoring, the associated address of well-known trader jez established a 5x leveraged long position in TAO on March 25, with a notional value exceeding $2.7 million. The MACD turned positive in March, and short sellers are being squeezed. However, before the second halving at the end of 2026, if the external revenue of subnets does not increase significantly, the exit of miners could lead to a substantial reduction in hashrate. Bittensor's high beta also means that if the Federal Reserve continues its hawkish stance or the situation in the Middle East spirals out of control, institutional funds will withdraw from these overvalued assets immediately. In summary, Bittensor is either building a decentralized AI "intelligent internet" or constructing a beautiful castle in the air using inflation. The difference between these two outcomes may only be a halving cycle. Jensen Huang said that future factories will produce tokens. But factories will also go bankrupt—especially those factories that can't sell their products and rely entirely on subsidies. Of TAO's $365, how much is the price of intelligence, and how much is the premium for faith? Nobody knows for sure right now.

data.title: The most important thing in Web3 primary market investment
data.description:
If Web3 from 2017 to 2021 was a blue ocean, then investing in the Web3 primary market from the end of 2021 to the end of 2025 can be described as a minefield. The investment logic and environment have shifted from simply being "driven by the project's future narrative" to "economic models, further scrutiny, and a dual drive of data and logic." The difficulty level has increased significantly, especially for primary capital. The CLiff policy, with its unlocking cycle similar to Web2, has greatly affected the activity of new projects. Among the 70 new projects listed on the largest cryptocurrency exchange in 2025, 95% showed a consistent downward trend. What caused this? Based on the current market environment, we will elaborate on several key judgment factors for early-stage rounds in this section.

1. The Essence of the Project: The Value Changes and Judgments of Entrepreneurs

In the Web3 field, the team is the only non-standardized variable and the soul of early-stage projects. Judging entrepreneurs requires not only looking at their impressive resumes but also penetrating their "crypto-native" level, the authenticity of their technical deliverables, and their moral bottom line in the face of extreme interests.

Since many projects in the primary market are still in their nascent stages, the capabilities, vision, and professional ethics of the entrepreneurial team are crucial. Having a keen eye for talent has become one of the most important talents and abilities for investors in this sector. GitHub commit frequency: Don't just listen to the technical vision in the pitch deck. In the AI ​​era, many business plans are even generated by AI with a single click, filled with AI fantasies. For technology-driven projects (such as Infra, ZK, L2), it is essential to have a technical partner or other technical institution review the underlying code on GitHub. However, since many early-stage projects haven't even started building their products yet, this step is highly dependent on the entrepreneur's past abilities and reputation. For example, with Algorand's technological vision at the time, knowing that an MIT Turing Award winner was at the helm of the project's underlying technology naturally lowered the difficulty of your technical due diligence. But by 2026, as the number of established, high-quality projects that could easily attract newcomers decreased, and the number of young entrepreneurs starting new projects increased, the requirements for judging people and conducting technical due diligence reached a new level. **Activity Trap:** Be wary of projects that suddenly commit code before securing funding but remain inactive for extended periods afterward. Data from 2025 shows that projects without audits or with chronically inactive codebases have an 80% higher risk of rg pulls. **Contributor Diversity:** Check whether the code was submitted by a single person or has diverse contributors. Large projects maintained by a single person often imply a very high "bus factor" risk. **While there are more OPC companies in the AI ​​era, the oversight and security risk screening by senior technical personnel for large projects remains crucial.** **Risks of Hiring Executives with Web2 Experience:** Be wary of teams with "parachuted" executives who have impeccable resumes at major Web2 companies but no prior experience in the Web3 blockchain. These types of hires can easily bring bad management practices from Web2 to the Web3 ecosystem, leading to incompatibility and hindering the project's later operation. Unless other members of the team have extensive Web3 experience to mitigate the risks, the risk is too high in the 2025-2026 period. Web3's decentralized governance and community culture are vastly different from Web2, and these hires often lack practical experience in handling community crises (such as governance attacks, fork threats, and economic models). Serial Entrepreneurs: In-depth Attribution of Past Failures: Serial entrepreneurs enjoy a premium in Web3, but it's crucial to distinguish between "honorable failures," "malicious exploitation," and "pure novices." Soft Rug Detection: Investigate whether the previous project experienced a soft rug, i.e., the team stopped development after fundraising citing "poor market conditions" without returning the funds. Treasury Misuse History: Examine the multisignature wallet records of previous projects to confirm whether there was any misappropriation of public funds for personal investment or high-risk financial management without community voting.

2. Judging the resources behind the project

Around 2026, the primary market of the Web3 industry has also entered a new stage. There are some confusing points. In the past year, major exchanges have frequently listed meme and sentiment-based coins, resulting in many projects with underlying technology and less emphasis on marketing not being favored.
